title: unpublished
text:
Course Website
My background is in Biomedical Engineering, and my research interests include:

Cardiovascular Mechanobiology and Tissue Engineering
Data Science Education in Low-Resource Settings
Accessible Data Visualization

I am also a Certified Carpentries Instructor and a trainer with the Digital Research Academy. In these roles my goal is to train learners on the basics of computational thinking. Additionally, I am an ardent promoter of using open-source tools (such as R/Python) to solve data analysis and visualization problems.

title: About
text: This is the teaching website for William Okech

title: Hello!
text:
Hi there! Welcome to my teaching, training, and research website.

Teaching
This is a repository for all my current teaching materials.


Training
In addition to university teaching, I am also a Certified Carpentries Instructor and a trainer with the Digital Research Academy. As a member of these organizations, my goal is to train learners on the basics of computational thinking and promote the use of open-source tools (such as R/Python) to solve data analysis and visualization problems.


Research Interests

Vascular Biology

Vascular Tissue Engineering
Endothelial cell mechanotransduction
Hemodynamics/Cardiovascular Fluid Mechanics

Biomedical Computing and Data Science Education for Low-Resource Settings

Context-aware biomedical data science training
Mobile dashboard interfaces for low-literacy users
Multimodal data visualizations for visually impaired users
Low-cost data science platforms for biomedical education

Sports Analytics

Wearable sensor data analysis and visualization
Low-cost platforms for athlete performance monitoring
Predictive modeling of athlete performance using physiological and training data

title: Sample Biomaterials Syllabus
text:
Prerequisites: TBD
Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Laboratory Sessions: TBD
Instructor: William Okech, PhD
Email Me
Course Website: https://wokech.github.io –> Courses –> Select Course


Biomaterials play an important role in the life sciences and medicine. They are used to aid in the restoration of function and promote healing after injury and disease. Biomaterials can be of natural, synthetic, or hybrid origin, and they are designed to interact with living systems in a safe and compatible manner. In this course, we will look at various materials including metals, ceramics, plastics, and composites, as well as living cells and tissues that are used to develop biomaterials. Additionally, the course will explore material characterization and testing methods, biocompatibility, and important considerations for the design and manufacture of materials for biomedical applications. In addition to lectures (where relevant), laboratory activities and demonstrations will be performed. Lastly, case studies and contemporary examples will be used to explore new technologies.




History and Current Uses of Biomaterials
Atomic Theory of Materials
Mechanics of Materials
Surface vs Bulk Properties of Materials
Biomaterial-Host Tissue Interactions
Material Degradation
Major Biomaterial Groups (Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, and Composites)
Phase Diagrams and Phase Transformations
Drug Delivery Systems and Tissue Engineering





Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Laboratory Exercises: TBD





Regular Examination at end of Semester: TBD
Continuous Assessment: TBD
The students must complete and pass all the labs before they can be allowed to proceed to the next class.

title: Biomechanics I (EBM 403)
text:
Prerequisites: Mechanics, physiology, anatomy
Lectures: TBD
Tutorials: TBD
Instructor: William Okech, PhD
Email Me
Course Website: https://wokech.github.io/courses.html –> select your course/year


Biomechanics is the application of the principles of mechanics (both solid and fluid) to biological systems and living organisms. These biological systems range from whole organisms to cells and tissues, as well as proteins. Various subfields of biomechanics exist including but not limited to cardiovascular, forensic, sports, comparative, and computational. In this course, we will introduce the basic concepts of biomechanics with a focus on the musculoskeletal system. Moreover, the learners will explore the mechanics of cells and tissues in the musculoskeletal system, the design of principles of orthopedic implants, and injury biomechanics. In addition to lectures (where relevant), laboratory activities and demonstrations will be performed. Lastly, case studies and contemporary examples will be used to explore new technologies.




Biomechanics of Joints
Ergonomics
Work Physiology and Industrial Safety
Basics of Fluid Mechanics
Prosthetics and Orthotics
Artificial Joints and Implants





Lectures: 2 hours per week
Tutorials: 2 hours per week





Regular Examination at end of Semester: 70%
Continuous Assessment: 30% where 10% shall be continuous assessment tests, 5% shall be assignments, and 15% shall be labs.
The students must complete and pass all the labs before they can be allowed to proceed to the next class.

Tissue Engineering: Part C

Journal of Applied Polymers
Journal of Controlled Release
Journal of Functional Biomaterials
Journal of Biomaterials Applications
Drug Delivery and Translational Research

Societies

Society of Biomaterials

Australasian Society for Biomaterials and Tissue Engineering

ASAIO (formerly known as the American Society for Artificial Internal Organs)
